metadata.summary: Agentic workflow for event-sourcing scaffolding: 5-stage pipeline, AI agent gotchas, subagent recommendations, prompt patterns, CLI tools, and hosted event store services.

content.title: Event Sourcing — Agentic Workflow and Tooling
content.summary: Drive event-sourcing scaffolding as a five-stage agentic pipeline: aggregate design → code-gen → projection → tests → anti-pattern review. Key gotchas: agents emit CRUD-style events, mutate state outside apply, and forget expected_version. Prompt patterns and CLI tools listed.

why.p: Event sourcing has a large surface area of interconnected decisions — event design, aggregate boundaries, projection shapes, versioning, PII strategy. An unguided agent produces CRUD-style snapshot events, aggregate methods that mutate state directly, repositories without optimistic concurrency, and projections that execute business logic. A structured five-stage pipeline with explicit anti-pattern gates produces correct scaffolding on the first pass.

list.item: Scaffolding a new event-sourced aggregate from a bounded context spec.
Reviewing a PR adding event-sourcing code against the anti-pattern checklist.
Generating projection, repository, and test scaffolding for an existing aggregate design.
Designing event boundaries for an unfamiliar domain — DDD aggregate design requires human domain expertise; the agent assists but cannot own the decisions.
Deleting or renaming published event classes — always human-reviewed, never auto-merged.

section.name: five-stage-pipeline
section.p: Drive event-sourcing scaffolding as a five-stage pipeline: (1) an aggregate-design agent identifies bounded context and events from the spec; (2) a code-gen agent emits Event classes (immutable, with event_id, occurred_at, aggregate_id, version), aggregate root with apply() and replay(events), repository with load(id) and save(events, expected_version); (3) a projection agent emits a projector per query with position tracking and idempotent UPSERT; (4) a test agent emits given/when/then tests where given = list[Event], when = command, then = list[Event]; (5) a review agent runs anti-pattern checks (mutable events, setattr on aggregates outside apply, missing expected_version on save). Persist event catalog in .aidocs/product_docs/event-catalog.md. Use faion-sdd-executor-agent to bind one event/projection/replay-test triple per SDD task.

section.name: agent-gotchas
section.p: These are the most frequent errors AI agents make when generating event-sourcing code. Each requires a specific lint rule or prompt constraint to prevent.

antipattern.description: CRUD-shaped events: agent emits UserUpdated, OrderUpdated, OrderUpdated_v2 — flat state snapshots.
antipattern.reason: Force naming "what business action happened" and reject Updated/Changed patterns in lint. Snapshot events destroy the audit trail — they carry no business intent.

antipattern.description: Mutating state outside apply: agent calls self.status = "placed" in command handlers because it feels natural.
antipattern.reason: The aggregate must mutate state ONLY in apply handlers. Command handlers emit events; replay invokes apply. State mutation in command methods breaks replay determinism.

antipattern.description: Forgotten expected_version: code-gen agent saves events without optimistic-concurrency check. Replays clobber concurrent writes.
antipattern.reason: Always include expected_version parameter in repository save signatures. The lint check for missing expected_version in event-catalog-lint.py catches this pattern.

antipattern.description: Hallucinated event-store APIs: agents invent event_store.append_one(...), repo.put_events(...), bus.fire(...) that do not exist in the target library.
antipattern.reason: Pin the exact API in the prompt and provide the imports. Include a concrete example of the target library's append() call signature.

antipattern.description: Snapshot misuse: agent persists current state as a snapshot AND treats it as truth, skipping event replay "if snapshot exists". This breaks event-versioning.
antipattern.reason: Always rebuild snapshot when event schema bumps. Snapshots are a performance cache, never the source of truth. Replay from events after the snapshot version must always be performed.

antipattern.description: Projection writes business logic: agent writes if order.total > 1000: send_email(...) in projection handler.
antipattern.reason: Projections are passive UPSERTs. Emit a domain event for "high-value order" and drive the action from a separate handler. Business logic in projections fires on every rebuild.

antipattern.description: PII leaks into events: agent copies command payload directly into events including shipping_address, email, card_token.
antipattern.reason: Force a redact step and a per-event PII-classification field. Lint blocks PII without a shred plan. See event-sourcing-versioning for crypto-shredding and PII externalization strategies.

antipattern.description: Cross-aggregate handlers: agent writes place_order(...) that loads Customer, Inventory, Payment, and mutates all four.
antipattern.reason: That is a saga, not a command handler. Refactor into a process manager. Cross-aggregate mutations are not atomic and produce ghost states.

antipattern.description: Tests on aggregate getters: agent tests aggregate.status == "placed" after replay instead of testing event emission.
antipattern.reason: Better: test that apply(events) produces the same state as the original aggregate (round-trip) and that command produces the expected events list. This tests the actual event-sourcing contract.

section.name: prompt-patterns
section.prompt: You are an event-sourcing architect. Given the bounded context spec in <spec>, output: 1. The aggregate root name and invariants. 2. A list of events as `<EventName>(field: type, ...)`. Events MUST include event_id (UUID), occurred_at (UTC datetime), aggregate_id, and aggregate_version. Events MUST be past tense and minimal -- no CRUD-style snapshots. 3. A list of commands; each command produces 1+ events on success or raises a domain exception. 4. The reconstruct rule: for each event, the aggregate field updates in `apply(<EventName>)`. Output as markdown tables. Reject events that look like state snapshots ("OrderUpdated with full Order").

You are reviewing a PR adding event-sourcing code. Flag any of: (1) Event class with non-frozen dataclass / mutable field, (2) aggregate method that mutates state outside its apply handler, (3) repository.save without expected_version (optimistic concurrency), (4) projection without last_processed_position tracking or non-idempotent write, (5) command handler that loads aggregate without replaying events from store, (6) edit to a published event class (in event catalog) without an upcaster + version bump, (7) PII in event payload without crypto-shredding plan. Cite file:line. Do not propose fixes.

section.name: subagent-recommendations
section.p: Recommended subagents for event-sourcing scaffolding tasks:

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — opus model fits because event-design choices (granularity, payload, versioning) are decision-heavy and irreversible. Use sequential mode: event → aggregate apply → command handler → projection → test, in that order.

password-scrubber-agent — run before committing event fixtures; events love to carry PII (shipping_address, card_token) into test snapshots and JSON dumps. Also useful before publishing event schemas to schema registry.

event-versioning-gate agent (worth adding under agents/): blocks PRs that modify a published event class without an upcaster and bump in event_version. Reads a .event-catalog.json to know which classes are published.
faion-feature-executor skill — sequential mode for ES feature slices: event → aggregate apply → command handler → projection → test, in that order.
For CQRS pairing, use sibling pro/dev/software-developer/cqrs-pattern/ and share the inventory file.
Events are past tense, intent-rich, minimal. OrderPlaced(order_id, customer_id, items, placed_at) not OrderUpdated(...full snapshot...). Reject "Updated/Changed" event names.
Frozen dataclasses (Python) / records (Java) / immutable C#. Compile-time guarantee that an event is never mutated after creation.
Stable event IDs from the producer. UUIDv7 (time-ordered) so projections can dedup and order across consumers.
Optimistic concurrency on append. repository.save(stream_id, events, expected_version) — fail fast on conflict, retry the command from the new state. Without this you get ghost writes.
Projections track position and are idempotent. (stream_id, position) PK on the projection's checkpoint table; UPSERT on apply.
Snapshot every N events (50–100), but treat as cache. Always able to reconstruct from event 0; snapshots are a perf optimization, never source of truth.
PII out of events or crypto-shred. Either reference customer_id (and store PII in a mutable side table that respects GDPR) or encrypt PII fields per-subject and discard the key on erasure request.
Event catalog is committed source. A .event-catalog.json lives in the repo; CI fails on incompatible changes.
One event per command success. Multi-event commands are a smell — usually the command should be split into two commands.
Replay tests as default. given_events → when_command → then_events is the canonical test shape; agents naturally produce them when the prompt enforces it.
Schedule projection rebuilds. Nightly job rebuilds projections from offset 0 in a shadow DB and diffs against live; alerts on drift before users notice.

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: Key CLI tools for event-sourced systems:

list.item: eventstore CLI / es — Append, read, manage projections on EventStoreDB. https://www.eventstore.com/eventstoredb
eventsourcing (Python) — Aggregate / repository / event-store framework. pip install eventsourcing. https://eventsourcing.readthedocs.io
axon-cli — Inspect / replay events on Axon Server. https://docs.axoniq.io
kafka-console-consumer / kcat — Inspect Kafka-backed event streams, replay from offset. https://github.com/edenhill/kcat
nats CLI + JetStream — Lightweight event store for small footprints. https://nats.io
Marten CLI tasks — Postgres-backed CQRS+ES; dotnet marten projection rebuild. https://martendb.io
Debezium — CDC from RDBMS to event log when refit is not an option. https://debezium.io
dbt — Build read-model projections as SQL models from append-only event tables. https://docs.getdbt.com
faktory / Temporal CLI — Saga / process manager orchestration around event store. https://temporal.io

section.name: services
section.p: Hosted and self-hosted event store services:

list.item: EventStoreDB (OSS + cloud) — Purpose-built event store; HTTP+gRPC APIs; built-in projections. Agent-friendly.
Axon Server (commercial + free dev tier) — Bus + event store for JVM stacks. Agent-friendly.
Marten (Postgres, OSS) — ES on Postgres; single-DB simplicity, ideal for small teams. Agent-friendly.
Apache Kafka + Schema Registry (OSS / Confluent SaaS) — Event log + projection sinks; high-throughput. Agent-friendly.
NATS JetStream (OSS / Synadia SaaS) — Subjects = aggregate streams; cheap to self-host. Agent-friendly.
AWS DynamoDB Streams + Kinesis (SaaS) — DDB as event store with Streams as bus; popular serverless ES pattern. Agent-friendly.
MessageDB / Eventide (Postgres, OSS) — Postgres-backed event store with PG NOTIFY-based subscriptions. Agent-friendly.
Apache Pulsar (OSS / StreamNative) — Multi-tenant event log; tiered storage solves long retention. Agent-friendly.
Temporal (OSS / Cloud) — Owns saga / process-manager layer that ES handlers must NOT own. Agent-friendly.
Apicurio / Confluent Schema Registry (SaaS / OSS) — Event schema versioning + compatibility checks; non-optional once more than 1 consumer exists. Agent-friendly.
